title: What Your June 30 Financials Are Really Telling a Future Buyer
linkedin:
Your June 30 accounts will be read by a future buyer. Most business owners don't realise that until they're already in the room.

Buyers build a normalised earnings figure from your accounts, adjusting for owner salary set at a non-market rate, personal expenses run through the business, and genuine one-off costs that won't repeat. Each defensible adjustment lifts that number. The multiple is applied to that number, not the one in the accounts.

For a business reporting $300,000 in profit, legitimate add-backs might produce a normalised figure of $420,000. At a five-times multiple, that difference in enterprise value is $600,000. Buyers will run those adjustments either way. The question is whether your records are clean enough to defend each one.

Beyond the earnings figure, buyers look at whether revenue has grown steadily across three years, whether cash conversion tracks the reported profit, and whether the business can run credibly without the current owner present. That last point is where the most value tends to sit, or not.

The year is not yet closed. There is still time to tidy aged receivables, review personal expenses running through the accounts, and note any genuine one-off costs with a brief management comment. These steps take hours. Their effect on the readability of your accounts during due diligence is material.

The period right after EOFY, when twelve months of clean financials are available, is the natural time for a first business valuation. Understanding where the number sits today makes it possible to move it deliberately over the years before a sale.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

June 30 is weeks away. Most business owners know it as the deadline for tax. Buyers know it as the moment your accounts take their final shape for the year.

The three years of financials a buyer reviews first tell a story about revenue consistency, cash quality, and how dependent the business is on you personally. That story sets the ceiling on your multiple before any negotiation begins.

Have you thought about what your accounts are showing right now? Full post here: https://profit-pulse.com.au/what-your-june-30-financials-are-really-telling-a-future-buyer

#BusinessValuation #AustralianBusiness
google:
If you are an East Coast business owner considering a sale in the next two to seven years, the decisions you make before June 30 shape what a future buyer will read in the accounts that matter most to any valuation.

Buyers use the last three years of financial statements to build a picture of your normalised earnings, your cash conversion cycle, and how dependent the business is on you personally. That picture sets the multiple before any negotiation starts.

Before the financial year closes, three practical steps remain available. Review any personal or discretionary costs running through the business accounts so your trading position reads clearly. Chase aged receivables to present a stronger working capital position. Note any genuine one-off costs with a brief management comment, so they are easy to explain during due diligence.

The period right after EOFY is also the natural time to commission a business valuation, when twelve months of clean financials are available and the picture is fresh. Whether a sale is two years or seven years away, understanding where your value sits today is what makes it possible to lift it deliberately.

ProfitPulse works with owner-led businesses in Brisbane, Queensland, and across the East Coast at exactly this point in the cycle. If you want to understand what your financials are telling a buyer, and what it would take to improve that number, the next few weeks are the right time to start.

Read the full post: https://profit-pulse.com.au/what-your-june-30-financials-are-really-telling-a-future-buyer
